Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to dedicate around 25 hours a week, mostly listening to recorded lectures and podcasts while commuting or doing chores. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd replay explanations multiple times until concepts clicked. But my actual class attendance was terrible—I kept telling myself I'd catch up through the recordings, which didn't always work out as planned.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, listening to materials every morning, but then life would throw something at me and I'd fall behind. Deadlines definitely drove my intensity—I'd cram listening sessions back-to-back when assignments were due. The stress was constant, but I kept pushing through because giving up wasn't an option for me.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check deadlines or download audio files for offline listening. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in—there was something satisfying about earning points and seeing progress. I'd also log in after getting notifications about new content, though I'll admit I didn't always engage deeply once I was there. It was more about grabbing what I needed and getting out.
Q4: For a brief period, checking the gamified elements became almost like a habit—like checking social media. I'd log in to see my progress on the leaderboard or complete quick tasks for points. But that routine fell apart when my anxiety spiked and I started avoiding anything school-related. The gamification helped initially, but it couldn't compete with everything else going on in my head.
Q5: The recorded lectures were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I'd play them at 1.5x speed, rewind confusing parts, and really absorb the material that way. The discussion forums were also helpful when peers explained things in different ways—sometimes hearing another student's perspective made concepts click better than the professor's explanation. The gamified tasks kept me motivated when I was struggling.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs. My eyes would glaze over trying to read dense text, and I'd retain almost nothing. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice problems because they felt tedious without any audio component. If something wasn't in a format I could listen to, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my priority list and stayed there.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assessments or when it had that gamified element that made progress visible. I loved when completing something gave me points or unlocked new content—it made the effort feel rewarded. Activities that felt isolated or disconnected from my grade just seemed like busywork, especially when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones created this pressure that actually helped me push through my anxiety and get things done. With optional stuff, my brain would calculate whether the effort was worth it, and usually the answer was no. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they felt more like a game than work, so I'd actually do those.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during walks or while cooking. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts that explained similar concepts in different ways. My notes were all handwritten from what I heard, and I'd review those constantly. The LMS was just my source material, not where learning actually happened.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was intensely engaged with course material but never touched the LMS. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, discussing concepts with friends over text, or watching related videos online. My actual understanding was growing, but my login count stayed flat. The system couldn't see me pacing around my room, replaying audio and talking through problems out loud.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe five or six times over the following days. The LMS would show one access, but that file lived on my phone for weeks. Same with any audio resources—one download, countless listens. My offline engagement was way higher than anything the system could track.
Q12: My anxiety hit really hard around midterms, and suddenly everything felt impossible. I'd open the LMS and just freeze, overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. The stress was crushing even though I kept trying to bounce back. There were days when I couldn't bring myself to engage at all, even though I knew I was digging myself deeper into trouble.
Q13: The workload from other courses combined with my high stress levels created this perfect storm of avoidance. I'd be exhausted and anxious, and logging into the LMS felt like walking into a room full of my failures. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely. When I was mentally depleted, I'd do the bare minimum just to feel like I wasn't completely giving up.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions. I'd click through videos without really processing anything because my mind was racing with anxiety. There were sessions where I technically watched content but couldn't tell you a single thing afterward. My body was present but my brain had checked out completely.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were crucial. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd think about all the ways I could fail and just shut down. I'd tell myself I'd feel more prepared tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's frustrating because I'm resilient and I kept coming back, but that initial hurdle of starting was brutal every single time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only external structure. I'd suddenly become hyper-focused in the days before something was due, cramming in all the listening and work I'd been avoiding. The gamified tasks with deadlines were easier to manage because they felt less intimidating. But the big assignments? I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable before I could force myself to start.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I got it. Seeing that I understood something correctly gave me a boost that carried me through harder sections. The gamified feedback—points, badges, progress bars—kept me engaged even when the academic feedback was sparse. But when feedback was delayed or absent, I felt lost and my engagement dropped significantly.
Q18: The course structure was honestly pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my situation. I needed more flexibility to engage with content in my own way, at my own pace. The gamified elements added some structure that felt supportive rather than punishing. But the strict attendance expectations and assignment schedule didn't account for students like me who were struggling but still trying.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. The gamification was genuinely helpful for my motivation. But the heavy reliance on reading materials and the lack of flexibility in deadlines made things harder. When I fell behind, there was no good way to catch up without feeling like I was drowning. More scaffolding for struggling students would have helped.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity can mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to translate that effort into completed work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, or they're engaging with content but not in ways that produce submissions. Sometimes clicking around the LMS is easier than facing the vulnerability of turning something in to be judged.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some students are efficient—they grab what they need and study effectively offline. Or they have strong foundational knowledge and don't need to engage as much with supplementary materials. The LMS activity doesn't measure understanding, just clicking. Someone could be brilliant and barely log in because they learn better from textbooks or study groups.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the invisible learning. They can't see me listening to a downloaded lecture while walking my dog, or the hours I spent discussing concepts with friends. Logs show behavior, not understanding or effort or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally invested in understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious about concepts, when I find myself thinking about course content outside of study time. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful, even if it's hard. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether the material is actually taking root in my mind.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the resilience it took to keep coming back after every setback. It doesn't show my offline listening sessions, my conversations with peers, or the genuine understanding I developed despite my terrible metrics. My engagement was real, even if the data doesn't prove it.
